questions.question: The Taj Mahal was built by which Mughal emperor?
questions.explanation: Shah Jahan built the Taj Mahal in Agra (1632–1653) in memory of his wife Mumtaz Mahal. It is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

questions.question: Which Mughal emperor is known as 'Akbar the Great'?
questions.explanation: Akbar (1556–1605) is regarded as one of the greatest Mughal emperors known for religious tolerance, administrative reforms, and expanding the empire.

questions.question: Which was the first capital of the Mughal Empire in India?
questions.explanation: Agra was the first capital of the Mughal Empire in India. Babur established it after the First Battle of Panipat (1526).

questions.question: The First Battle of Panipat (1526) was fought between:
questions.explanation: The First Battle of Panipat (1526) was fought between Babur (founder of Mughal Empire) and Ibrahim Lodi (last ruler of Delhi Sultanate). Babur's victory established Mughal rule in India.

questions.question: The Partition of Bengal (1905) was annulled in:
questions.explanation: The Partition of Bengal (1905) by Lord Curzon was annulled in 1911 under Lord Hardinge following massive protests. The capital was shifted from Calcutta to Delhi.

questions.question: Tipu Sultan was the ruler of which kingdom?
questions.explanation: Tipu Sultan (1750–1799) was the Sultan of Mysore. He was called the 'Tiger of Mysore' for his fierce resistance against British expansion. He died in the Fourth Anglo-Mysore War.

questions.question: The Pandya dynasty's capital was:
questions.explanation: Madurai was the capital of the Pandya dynasty throughout history — from Sangam Age through medieval period. Ancient Madurai is described in detail in Sangam literature (Maturaikkanci).

questions.question: Hampi was the capital of:
questions.explanation: Hampi (Karnataka) was the capital of the Vijayanagara Empire. It is now a UNESCO World Heritage Site with magnificent ruins including Virupaksha Temple, Vittala Temple, and palace structures.

questions.question: The Indian National Army trials (INA trials, 1945) were held at:
questions.explanation: The INA trials were held at Red Fort, Delhi (November 1945), charging INA officers with treason against the British. The trials created massive public outrage that unified Indians.

questions.question: Sher Shah Suri introduced the currency called:
questions.explanation: Sher Shah Suri (1540-1545) introduced the silver rupee (rupiya) weighing 178 grains. He also introduced copper paisa and gold mohur — currency reforms that continued under Mughals.

questions.question: Who was the author of 'Baburnama'?
questions.explanation: Baburnama (Tuzuk-i-Baburi) was written by Babur himself in Chagatai Turkish — it is the first autobiography in Islamic literary tradition, describing his life, campaigns, and observations of India.

questions.question: Ibadat Khana (House of Worship) was established by:
questions.explanation: Akbar established Ibadat Khana (House of Worship) at Fatehpur Sikri in 1575 CE for religious debates among scholars of different faiths — precursor to his Din-i-Ilahi.

questions.question: The Battle of Khanwa (1527) was between:
questions.explanation: Battle of Khanwa (17 March 1527): Babur defeated Rana Sanga (Rana Sangram Singh) of Mewar, who had formed a coalition of Rajput chiefs to challenge Babur.

questions.question: Akbar defeated Hemu in:
questions.explanation: Second Battle of Panipat (5 November 1556): Akbar's general Bairam Khan defeated and killed Hemu (the last Hindu emperor to sit on Delhi's throne briefly), securing Mughal rule.

questions.question: The treaty that ended the Third Anglo-Mysore War was:
questions.explanation: Treaty of Seringapatam (1792) ended the Third Anglo-Mysore War — Tipu Sultan surrendered half his territory and paid large indemnity. The Fourth War (1799) ended with Tipu's death.

questions.question: Tipu Sultan died defending:
questions.explanation: Tipu Sultan (Sultan of Mysore) died on 4 May 1799 defending his capital Srirangapatna against British forces. His death ended the kingdom of Mysore.

questions.question: The Mughal emperor who was also called 'Zinda Pir' (Living Saint):
questions.explanation: Aurangzeb was called 'Zinda Pir' (Living Saint) for his piety — he fasted regularly, never drank alcohol, lived simply, and supplemented his income by copying Qurans and making prayer caps.

questions.question: In Indian history, 'Second Battle of Tarain (1192)' established:
options: Permanent Muslim rule in North India
questions.explanation: Second Battle of Tarain (1192) — Muhammad Ghori's defeat of Prithviraj Chauhan — established permanent Muslim rule in North India, beginning the Delhi Sultanate period.

questions.question: The Bahmani Kingdom was founded by:
questions.explanation: Alauddin Bahman Shah (Hassan Gangu, 1347-1358) founded the Bahmani Kingdom after revolting against Muhammad bin Tughluq of Delhi. It split into 5 Deccan Sultanates after 1518.

questions.question: Akbar's 'Sulh-i-Kul' policy means:
questions.explanation: Sulh-i-Kul (Peace with all/Absolute peace) was Akbar's policy of universal tolerance — treating all religions equally, respecting all faiths without discrimination.

questions.question: Which Mughal emperor translated the Ramayana and Mahabharata into Persian?
questions.explanation: Akbar had the Ramayana, Mahabharata, and other Sanskrit texts translated into Persian, making them accessible to Persian-speaking scholars and promoting cultural synthesis.

questions.question: The first Sultan of Delhi was:
questions.explanation: Qutb-ud-din Aibak (1206–1210 CE) was the first Sultan of Delhi and founder of the Mamluk (Slave) dynasty. He was a slave of Muhammad Ghori. He started the Qutb Minar and the Quwwat-ul-Islam mosque.

questions.question: The five dynasties of the Delhi Sultanate in order were:
questions.explanation: Delhi Sultanate (1206–1526): 1) Mamluk/Slave dynasty (1206–1290), 2) Khalji dynasty (1290–1320), 3) Tughlaq dynasty (1320–1414), 4) Sayyid dynasty (1414–1451), 5) Lodi dynasty (1451–1526).

questions.question: Iltutmish is considered the true founder of the Delhi Sultanate because:
options: He consolidated the Sultanate, got recognition from Caliph of Baghdad, completed Qutb Minar, and introduced iqta system
questions.explanation: Iltutmish (1210–1236) got recognition from the Abbasid Caliph (Islamic legitimacy), introduced the iqta system (land revenue assignments for military service), completed the Qutb Minar (started by Aibak), and introduced silver tanka and copper jital coins.

questions.question: Muhammad bin Tughlaq's controversial decision to shift the capital from Delhi to Daulatabad (Devagiri) was:
options: A disaster — he forced the entire population of Delhi to relocate, causing mass deaths; later reversed
Done at the request of Sufi saints
questions.explanation: Muhammad bin Tughlaq (1325–1351) shifted the capital from Delhi to Daulatabad (1327) to better control the Deccan. The forced migration caused suffering and deaths. The plan failed — he had to shift back. Ibn Battuta documented this.

questions.question: The invasion of Timur (Tamerlane) devastated Delhi in:
questions.explanation: Timur (Tamerlane) of Samarkand invaded India and sacked Delhi in 1398 CE, massacring 100,000+ prisoners and looting enormous wealth. This destroyed the Tughlaq dynasty and led to the Sayyid and Lodi dynasties.

questions.question: The last Sultan of Delhi was:
questions.explanation: Ibrahim Lodi (1517–1526) was the last Sultan of Delhi. He was defeated and killed at the First Battle of Panipat (1526) by Babur, ending the Delhi Sultanate and beginning the Mughal Empire.

questions.question: The Vijayanagara Empire was founded by:
options: Harihara I and Bukka Raya I (with guidance of sage Vidyaranya)
questions.explanation: The Vijayanagara Empire was founded in 1336 CE by brothers Harihara I and Bukka Raya I (formerly commanders of the Kampili kingdom) with the spiritual guidance of sage Vidyaranya (Madhava). Capital: Hampi (Vijayanagara).

questions.question: The greatest ruler of the Vijayanagara Empire was:
questions.explanation: Krishna Deva Raya (1509–1529 CE) was the greatest Vijayanagara ruler. He defeated Bijapur, Bidar, Golconda, and the Zamorin of Calicut. He was a poet (wrote Amuktamalyada in Telugu) and patron of Ashtadiggajas (eight court poets).

questions.question: The Battle of Talikota (1565) resulted in:
options: Vijayanagara victory over the Deccan Sultanates
Destruction of the Vijayanagara Empire — the capital Hampi was sacked and ruined
questions.explanation: The Battle of Talikota (Rakshasatangadi, 1565) was fought between Vijayanagara (Aliya Rama Raya) and the allied Deccan Sultanates (Bijapur, Ahmadnagar, Bidar, Golconda). Rama Raya was killed, Hampi was devastated and never recovered.

questions.question: Babur founded the Mughal Empire after winning:
questions.explanation: Babur (Zahir-ud-din Muhammad) defeated Ibrahim Lodi at the First Battle of Panipat (21 April 1526) using gunpowder and artillery (tulugma tactics). This established the Mughal Empire. He then defeated Rana Sanga at Khanwa (1527).

questions.question: The founder of the Slave Dynasty was:
questions.explanation: Qutb-ud-din Aibak (1206-1210 CE) founded the Slave (Mamluk) Dynasty — the first Muslim dynasty in India. He was a slave of Muhammad Ghori who rose to become a sultan.

questions.question: The first woman ruler of the Delhi Sultanate was:
questions.explanation: Razia Sultana (1236-1240 CE), daughter of Iltutmish, was the first woman ruler of the Delhi Sultanate. She discarded purdah and held court openly.

questions.question: Muhammad bin Tughluq's transfer of capital from Delhi to Daulatabad was:
questions.explanation: Muhammad bin Tughluq (1327 CE) transferred the capital from Delhi to Daulatabad (Devagiri) in the Deccan for better control of the empire, but it failed — the northern areas became unmanageable.

questions.question: Ibn Battuta visited India during the reign of:
questions.explanation: Ibn Battuta (Moroccan traveler) visited India 1333-1347 CE during the reign of Muhammad bin Tughluq, who appointed him as Qazi of Delhi. His book Rihla describes India in detail.

questions.question: The 'iqta' system in Delhi Sultanate was similar to:
options: Feudal land grants for military service
questions.explanation: Iqta was a grant of land revenue (not land itself) to military officers (iqtadars) in lieu of salary, in return for maintaining troops and providing military service.

questions.question: The greatest ruler of Vijayanagara Empire was:
questions.explanation: Krishnadevaraya (1509-1529 CE) was the greatest ruler — a brilliant military commander, poet (wrote Amuktamalyada in Telugu), and generous patron of arts. Known as Kannada Rajya Rama Ramana.

questions.question: Abdur Razzaq, the Persian ambassador, visited which Vijayanagara king's court?
questions.explanation: Abdur Razzaq (Persian envoy, 1443) visited the court of Devaraya II and gave a detailed description of Vijayanagara city and the empire's grandeur.

questions.question: Battle of Talikota (1565) ended:
questions.explanation: Battle of Talikota (Jan 1565): Vijayanagara defeated by the combined forces of Bijapur, Bidar, Ahmadnagar, and Golconda. The city of Vijayanagara was sacked and destroyed.

questions.question: Babur defeated Ibrahim Lodi at the First Battle of Panipat in:
questions.explanation: First Battle of Panipat (21 April 1526): Babur defeated Ibrahim Lodi using the Tulughma strategy and artillery, establishing the Mughal Empire in India.

questions.question: The Mughal emperor famous for building Taj Mahal:
questions.explanation: Shah Jahan (1628-1658) built the Taj Mahal at Agra (1632-1643) as a mausoleum for his beloved wife Mumtaz Mahal. It is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

questions.question: Aurangzeb reimposed the 'Jizya' tax which had been abolished by:
questions.explanation: Akbar abolished the discriminatory Jizya (poll tax on non-Muslims) in 1564 CE as part of his policy of religious tolerance. Aurangzeb reimposed it in 1679 CE.

questions.question: Todar Mal was the Finance Minister of:
questions.explanation: Raja Todar Mal was Akbar's revenue minister who introduced the Zabti (measurement) system of land revenue — measuring land and fixing revenue based on soil type and cultivation.

questions.question: The 'Mansabdari system' was a Mughal system of:
questions.explanation: Mansabdari system (introduced by Akbar) graded military and civil officials (mansabdars) by numerical ranks (mansabs) determining salary and cavalry obligation.

questions.question: The Battle of Haldighati (1576) was fought between:
questions.explanation: Battle of Haldighati (18 June 1576) was fought between Maharana Pratap of Mewar and the Mughal forces led by Man Singh I on Akbar's behalf. Pratap was defeated but never surrendered.

questions.question: The Battle of Buxar (1764) was significant because:
options: British defeated combined armies of three powers
First use of firearms in India
questions.explanation: Battle of Buxar (22 October 1764): British under Hector Munro defeated the combined forces of Mir Qasim (Bengal), Shuja-ud-Daula (Awadh), and Mughal Emperor Shah Alam II — establishing British dominance.

questions.question: The 'Ain-i-Akbari' was written by:
questions.explanation: Ain-i-Akbari (Institutes of Akbar) was written by Abul Fazl, Akbar's court historian. It is the third volume of Akbarnama, providing detailed accounts of Akbar's administration, policies, and the Mughal court.

questions.question: Akbar's 'Ain-i-Akbari' and 'Akbarnama' were written by:
questions.explanation: Abul Fazl (1551–1602), a minister and historian, wrote Akbarnama (in 3 volumes, including the Ain-i-Akbari). The Ain-i-Akbari is a detailed account of Akbar's administration, court, culture, and statistics of the empire.

questions.question: The Mughal land revenue system during Akbar's reign was called:
options: Dahsala or Zabti system (introduced by Todar Mal)
questions.explanation: Raja Todar Mal introduced the Dahsala (ten-year average) system: land was measured (zabt), soil quality classified into 4 types, and revenue fixed for 10 years based on average produce. It replaced earlier arbitrary systems.

questions.question: The partition of Bengal was annulled in:
questions.explanation: Lord Curzon partitioned Bengal in 1905. Due to massive protests (Swadeshi Movement), the partition was annulled in 1911 when the capital was shifted from Calcutta to Delhi.

questions.question: The INA (Indian National Army) was organized by:
questions.explanation: Subhas Chandra Bose reorganized the INA (Azad Hind Fauj) in 1943 in Singapore. 'Jai Hind', 'Dilli Chalo' were his rallying cries. Marched from Rangoon toward Delhi.

questions.question: Who was the founder of the Mughal Empire in India?
questions.explanation: Babur established the Mughal Empire after his victory in the First Battle of Panipat in 1526.

questions.question: The famous traveler Ibn Battuta came to India during the reign of which Delhi Sultan?
questions.explanation: Ibn Battuta, a Moroccan traveler, visited India during the reign of Muhammad bin Tughlaq in the 14th century.

questions.question: Which of the following Sufi saints is credited with popularizing the Chishtiya order in North India, establishing his Khanqah at Ajmer?
questions.explanation: Khwaja Muinuddin Chishti established the Chishtiya order in India with his center at Ajmer. Sheikh Nizamuddin Auliya was a prominent disciple of Baba Farid, who was a disciple of Khwaja Qutbuddin Bakhtiyar Kaki, who was a disciple of Khwaja Muinuddin Chishti.

questions.question: During the reign of which Delhi Sultanate ruler did Ibn Battuta, the Moroccan traveler, serve as a Qazi (judge) in Delhi?
questions.explanation: Ibn Battuta served as a Qazi in Delhi under Muhammad bin Tughlaq. He documented his extensive travels in his book 'Rihla'.

questions.question: The architectural style known as 'Indo-Saracenic' flourished significantly during the Mughal period, blending indigenous Indian elements with Islamic styles. Which of the following structures is an early example of this fusion, predating the high Mughal era?
questions.explanation: The Qutub Minar Complex, particularly the Quwwat-ul-Islam Mosque and the Alai Darwaza, showcases early Indo-Saracenic elements. Humayun's Tomb is a precursor to high Mughal architecture, while Red Fort and Fatehpur Sikri are from later Mughal periods.

questions.question: Which of the following land revenue systems, involving a measurement-based assessment and direct collection by the state, was primarily introduced and perfected by Sher Shah Suri before being adopted and further refined by Akbar?
questions.explanation: The Zabt System, a measurement-based assessment, was perfected by Sher Shah Suri and later adopted by Akbar. Batai and Ghallabakshi were other forms of revenue collection, while Nasaq was a rough assessment.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements regarding the Bhakti Movement in Medieval India:
1. It emphasized devotion (bhakti) as the primary means to attain salvation.
2. Many Bhakti saints preached in Sanskrit to reach a wider audience.
3. It generally challenged the caste system and promoted social equality.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Statement 1 and 3 are correct. Bhakti saints primarily preached in vernacular languages, not Sanskrit, to connect with the common people.

questions.question: Which of the following statements accurately reflects the impact of the Bhakti movement on the socio-political landscape of medieval India, particularly in relation to the Delhi Sultanate's administrative policies and the rise of regional kingdoms?
options: The Bhakti movement, by emphasizing devotionalism over ritualism, inadvertently weakened the Brahminical dominance, thereby creating a vacuum that was partially filled by Sufi orders patronized by certain Sultanate rulers seeking popular legitimacy, while also providing a spiritual bedrock for the emergence of independent regional identities.

The Bhakti movement directly challenged the theological foundations of the Delhi Sultanate, leading to widespread peasant uprisings that significantly destabilized the central administration and directly contributed to the rapid decline of Tughlaq dynasty.

Bhakti saints, primarily focused on social reform, actively collaborated with the Delhi Sultanate to implement land reforms and abolish the Jizya tax, which strengthened the Sultanate's hold over Hindu populations and prevented the formation of regional power centers.

The Bhakti movement was largely confined to intellectual elites and had minimal impact on the broader populace or the political dynamics, remaining a purely religious phenomenon distinct from the administrative and political struggles of the time.
questions.explanation: The Bhakti movement's emphasis on devotion and individual piety challenged traditional hierarchies, indirectly impacting Brahminical authority and aligning with Sufi inclusivity. This spiritual reorientation also fostered regional cultural identities, contributing to the context for the rise of regional kingdoms amidst a weakening Sultanate.

questions.question: Consider the architectural innovations under the Tughlaq dynasty in comparison to the earlier Khalji period. Which of the following statements best describes the distinct shift in architectural priorities and structural elements, and its underlying socio-economic and political motivations during Ghiyasuddin Tughlaq's reign?
options: Ghiyasuddin Tughlaq's architecture, exemplified by Tughlaqabad Fort, marked a departure from the ornamental grandeur of Khalji structures, prioritizing defensive strength and austere simplicity, reflecting a state facing constant external threats and internal dissent, necessitating practical, robust fortifications over lavish aesthetics.

The Tughlaq dynasty continued and enhanced the Khalji emphasis on intricate marble inlay and decorative motifs, but introduced the double dome, signifying a period of unparalleled economic prosperity and cultural synthesis under Muhammad bin Tughlaq's ambitious projects.

Tughlaq architecture saw a complete abandonment of Islamic architectural principles, adopting purely indigenous Indian styles as a deliberate move to integrate with Hindu subjects and legitimize their rule, a stark contrast to the Khalji's Persianate influences.

Ghiyasuddin Tughlaq, influenced by Sufi mystics, introduced the concept of 'floating mosques' constructed entirely from wood, symbolizing the ephemeral nature of worldly power, a radical departure from the stone and brick structures of the Khalji era.
questions.explanation: Ghiyasuddin Tughlaq prioritized defensive and austere architecture, reflecting a period of political instability and resource constraints. This contrasts sharply with the Khalji's more ornate and grand structures, indicative of different socio-political environments.

questions.question: Analyze the strategic implications of the political fragmentation following the decline of the Ghaznavid Empire in the Indian subcontinent. How did this fragmentation, coupled with the rise of various Rajput confederacies, ultimately pave the way for the eventual establishment and consolidation of the Ghurid Empire's authority in Northern India, particularly under Muhammad of Ghor?
options: The fragmented Rajput kingdoms, often engaged in internecine conflicts, failed to form a united front against the Ghurid incursions, allowing Muhammad of Ghor to exploit these divisions and conquer them individually, despite their individual military prowess.

The Ghaznavid decline created a power vacuum that was primarily filled by a strong, unified Rajput empire, which then successfully repelled the Ghurid invasions for over a century, delaying their establishment in India.

The political fragmentation led to a significant increase in trade routes and economic prosperity among the regional Indian kingdoms, making them too wealthy to be easily conquered by the Ghurids, who were primarily interested in plunder.

The decline of the Ghaznavids resulted in their immediate absorption into the strongest Rajput confederacy, which then formed an alliance with the Ghurids to jointly rule Northern India, thereby avoiding direct conflict.
questions.explanation: The political fragmentation of the Indian subcontinent, marked by constant infighting among Rajput kingdoms, prevented a unified resistance. This disunity allowed Muhammad of Ghor to systematically defeat them and establish Ghurid rule.

questions.question: Which of the following dynasties ruled Delhi immediately after the Mamluk (Slave) Dynasty?
questions.explanation: The Khilji Dynasty succeeded the Mamluk (Slave) Dynasty in the Delhi Sultanate. This transition marked a significant change in the ruling power.

questions.question: The famous traveler Ibn Battuta, who visited India during the medieval period, was from which country?
questions.explanation: Ibn Battuta was a Moroccan explorer who traveled extensively and provided valuable accounts of his journeys, including his time in India.

questions.question: Which of the following Delhi Sultanate rulers is credited with introducing the 'Dagh' (branding of horses) and 'Chehra' (descriptive roll of soldiers) systems to prevent corruption and maintain a strong army?
questions.explanation: Alauddin Khalji implemented the Dagh and Chehra systems to streamline his military administration and curb fraudulent practices. These reforms were crucial for maintaining the efficiency of his large army.

questions.question: The famous traveler Ibn Battuta, who visited India during the Tughlaq dynasty, was originally from which country?
questions.explanation: Ibn Battuta was a Moroccan explorer who served as a Qazi under Muhammad bin Tughlaq. His travelogue 'Rihla' provides valuable insights into medieval Indian society.

questions.question: The term 'Jizya' in medieval India primarily referred to a tax levied on whom?
questions.explanation: Jizya was a poll tax historically levied on non-Muslim subjects (dhimmis) in Islamic states. It was a common feature of many medieval Islamic empires, including those in India.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the Vijayanagara Empire is NOT correct?
options: It was founded by Harihara I and Bukka Raya I.
It successfully resisted the Bahmani Sultanate for a long period.
Its capital, Hampi, was known for its elaborate temples and architecture.
Krishnadevaraya was the last significant ruler of the Tuluva dynasty.
questions.explanation: Krishnadevaraya was a powerful ruler of the Tuluva dynasty, but he was not the last. The empire declined after his reign, but others followed.

questions.question: The 'Battle of Talikota' (1565 AD) led to the significant decline of which powerful South Indian kingdom?
questions.explanation: The Battle of Talikota was a decisive defeat for the Vijayanagara Empire against a confederacy of Deccan Sultanates. This battle severely weakened the empire and marked the beginning of its downfall.

questions.question: Which of the following statements accurately describes the interconnectedness of socio-religious movements and political consolidation during the Delhi Sultanate period, specifically highlighting the impact of Sufism on state legitimacy and administrative structure under the Tughlaqs?
options: Sufi orders, particularly the Chishtis, actively participated in state administration, holding prominent judicial positions and directly influencing fiscal policies, thereby strengthening the Tughlaq regime's control over rural populations.

The Tughlaq sultans, wary of the burgeoning influence of Sufi silsilahs, implemented policies to restrict their land grants (waqfs) and curtailed their proselytizing activities, viewing them as potential threats to their centralized authority.

Sufi saints, through their emphasis on egalitarianism and social justice, often critiqued the Tughlaq state's hierarchical structure and revenue demands, leading to popular uprisings that weakened the Sultanate's hold in peripheral regions.

While generally apolitical, the moral authority and popular appeal of certain Sufi pirs, like Nizamuddin Auliya, indirectly conferred a degree of spiritual legitimacy upon the Tughlaq rulers, even as the state occasionally sought to co-opt or control their influence through patronage and grants, impacting local governance.
questions.explanation: Sufi saints, particularly the Chishtis, maintained a distance from court politics but their moral authority indirectly legitimized rulers. Tughlaq rulers often sought to patronize or control Sufi institutions to enhance their own standing and influence local populations without direct administrative integration.

questions.question: Analyze the economic implications of the 'Jagirdari Crisis' during the later Mughal period, considering its impact on agricultural productivity and the subsequent rise of regional powers. Which of the following best explains the primary economic consequence and its political corollary?
options: The crisis led to an equitable redistribution of land among peasants, boosting agricultural output due to increased incentives, which in turn strengthened the central Mughal authority by reducing provincial dissent.

It resulted in an intensified exploitation of the peasantry by jagirdars seeking to maximize their short-term gains, leading to widespread agrarian distress, depopulation of villages, and a decline in agricultural production, thus weakening the Emperor's revenue base and empowering local chieftains.

The Jagirdari Crisis prompted the Mughals to implement extensive land reforms, converting jagirs into khalsa lands and directly collecting revenue, which stabilized the economy and prevented the emergence of independent regional states.

The crisis primarily affected urban trade and commerce due to a decline in demand from the nobility, but agricultural productivity remained largely unaffected, allowing regional powers to thrive on alternative economic activities.
questions.explanation: The Jagirdari Crisis led to over-exploitation of peasants and land, causing agricultural decline and revenue shortfalls. This weakened the central Mughal authority, enabling regional powers to assert independence.

questions.question: During the reign of Sultan Firoz Shah Tughlaq, a significant shift in state policy occurred regarding religious endowments and public works. Which statement accurately links his administrative reforms concerning these areas to the broader socio-economic and political objectives of his rule, particularly in contrast to his predecessors?
options: Firoz Shah Tughlaq abolished all existing waqfs and religious grants, diverting these funds to military expansion and infrastructure projects, thereby centralizing state control over religious institutions and creating a more secular administration.

He significantly increased state patronage for Islamic religious scholars (Ulama) and institutions, establishing numerous madrasas and hospitals, and codified Sharia law, aiming to solidify his legitimacy and foster social stability by aligning the state more closely with orthodox Islamic principles, while also implementing extensive irrigation and public works to boost agricultural revenue.

Firoz Shah Tughlaq initiated a policy of religious tolerance, actively funding temples and non-Islamic shrines, and decentralizing the administration of waqfs to local communities, intending to gain broader popular support and reduce religious tensions.

His reforms focused solely on military modernization and urban planning, neglecting religious endowments and public welfare, which led to widespread discontent among both the Ulama and the common populace, ultimately weakening the Sultanate.
questions.explanation: Firoz Shah Tughlaq emphasized Islamic orthodoxy and patronized Ulama to gain legitimacy. Concurrently, he undertook extensive public works, including irrigation, to enhance agricultural productivity and state revenue, a departure from some predecessors' more aggressive fiscal policies.

questions.question: The Delhi Sultanate was founded by
questions.explanation: Qutb-ud-din Aibak founded the Delhi Sultanate in 1192.

questions.question: The Vijayanagara Empire was founded in
questions.explanation: The Vijayanagara Empire was founded in 1336 by Harihara and Bukka Raya.

questions.question: The Battle of Talikota was fought in
questions.explanation: The Battle of Talikota was fought in 1565 between the Vijayanagara Empire and the Deccan Sultanates.

questions.question: The ruler who introduced the concept of Din-i-Ilahi
questions.explanation: Akbar introduced the concept of Din-i-Ilahi in 1581.

questions.question: The capital of the Pallava dynasty was
questions.explanation: The capital of the Pallava dynasty was Kanchipuram.

questions.question: The Chola dynasty was founded by
questions.explanation: The Chola dynasty was founded by Vijayalaya in 848 CE.

questions.question: The ruler who built the Qutub Minar
questions.explanation: Qutb-ud-din Aibak built the Qutub Minar.

questions.question: The Hoysala dynasty was founded in
questions.explanation: The Hoysala dynasty was founded in 1150 by Nripa Kama.

questions.question: The Kakatiya dynasty was founded by
questions.explanation: The Kakatiya dynasty was founded by Ganapati Deva.

questions.question: The Tughlaq dynasty was founded by
questions.explanation: The Tughlaq dynasty was founded by Ghiyas-ud-din Tughlaq in 1320.

questions.question: Who was the founder of the Tughlaq dynasty?
questions.explanation: Ghiyas-ud-din Tughlaq founded the Tughlaq dynasty in 1320. He was the first ruler of the dynasty.

questions.question: What was the name of the famous university established during the reign of the Delhi Sultanate?
questions.explanation: Madrasa-i-Firoz Shahi was a famous university established during the reign of the Delhi Sultanate. It was founded by Firoz Shah Tughlaq.

questions.question: Which Delhi Sultan introduced the token currency?
questions.explanation: Muhammad-bin-Tughlaq introduced the token currency. He introduced copper coins to replace gold and silver coins.

questions.question: Who built the Qutub Minar?
questions.explanation: Qutb-ud-din Aibak built the Qutub Minar. It was completed by his successor Iltutmish.

questions.question: What was the name of the market established by Alauddin Khalji?
questions.explanation: Alauddin Khalji established the Sarai Adil Market. It was a major market in Delhi.

questions.question: Who was the last ruler of the Delhi Sultanate?
questions.explanation: Ibrahim Lodi was the last ruler of the Delhi Sultanate. He was defeated by Babur in 1526.

questions.question: What was the name of the dynasty that succeeded the Tughlaq dynasty?
questions.explanation: The Sayyid dynasty succeeded the Tughlaq dynasty. It was founded by Khizr Khan.

questions.question: Who built the Hauz Khas?
questions.explanation: Muhammad-bin-Tughlaq built the Hauz Khas. It was a large tank in Delhi.
